data.title: Tesla: Distracted Company, Weak FSD (Double Downgrade)
data.description:
Co-Authored by Noah Cox and Brock Heilig

Investment Thesis

While Tesla reported earnings last week that slightly beat expectations, I’m double downgrading shares of Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) from a strong buy to a hold. As Musk noted on the call, car volumes and their margins are likely going through a year+ trough, it’s clear that the EV maker is now more distracted than ever. And even with their CEO (Musk) fully focused on the EV giant (DOGE is in the past), Tesla that is in a far worse position compared to even 12 months ago. This downgrade is due to the heavy emerging risks that outweigh my prior bullish view. Over the last month, Tesla shares are flat, and they’re down over 20% year-to-date.

In my opinion, the EV maker faces significant distractions at a pivotal time for Tesla’s operations- they really should be all in on FSD. There will be more in-depth coverage on this point later on in the article, but I do not believe that this is a good recipe for Tesla shareholders.

This distraction coincides with two consecutive quarters of year-over-year delivery declines to start off 2025. I believe that this undermines Tesla’s growth narrative. I think that the stock’s recent rally - it’s up about 35% since bottoming out in early April - has priced in flawless execution, leaving no margin for error if fundamentals wobble.

Fundamentals are now wobbling.

With this, I think shares of the electric vehicle maker are not a surefire success as they were when I last wrote on the company. Because of this, I am double downgrading shares from a strong buy to a hold.

Why I Am Doing Follow Up Coverage

Again, while earnings last week were a slight beat, one of the biggest worries I have is the stark disconnect between the ~35% rally from early April and the ~13% drop in H1 2025 vehicle deliveries compared to last year. According to YCharts, in Q1 and Q2 2024, Tesla delivered ~386,000 and ~444,000 units, respectively. Keep in mind that Q1 2024 was already a 20% decline in volume compared to 2023.

YCharts reports that in Q1 2025, Tesla delivered only 336,000 units, which was down about 50,000 from the previous year. CNBC reported that Tesla delivered approximately 384,000 units in Q2, making for an H1 total of 720,000, which is down, as I mentioned, about 13% compared to H1 2024.

I want to take a moment to reassess the direction Tesla is headed, because this has marked on-and-off delivery misses for over a year and a half now. In Q1, the company was projected to deliver between 377,000 and 390,000 units. They failed to meet this mark. In Q2, they only delivered 384,000 units, which missed the expected mark of 394,000. Management is losing control of the narrative here that they are a growth company.

Even longtime bulls like Wedbush’s Dan Ives publicly urged the board to rein in Musk. Ives recently wrote a note to clients titled, "The Tesla board MUST Act and Create Ground Rules For Musk; Soap Opera Must End.”

Below is an excerpt from that note:

Tesla is heading into one of the most important stages of its growth cycle with the autonomous and robotics future now on the doorstep and cannot have Musk spending more and more time creating a political party which will require countless time, energy, and political capital -Ives wrote.

I’m revisiting Tesla because the company’s valuation has ballooned - the forward P/E is up above 190x - and operational cracks are widening.

Really, Tesla’s valuation right now hinges on FSD rollout (I believe). This is also one of the best places to see that the company as a whole is distracted. The results have been less than optimal for shareholders.

Q2 Review

While there has been a lot of great analysis since Q2 earnings (and this analysis is not focused on just Q2), I want to use Q2 as an example to show how the company is still (and even more) distracted than before.

My favorite example from the Q2 call is the Tesla diner:

We also launched the Tesla Diner, which has been a huge hit. It actually got worldwide attention, which is unusual for a diner. Diners don't typically get headline news around the earth. But this is a pretty special diner.

To be clear, I get why Tesla did this (to draw buzz to their brand and vehicles) but this doesn’t do anything to really adequately address their FSD status (which is what I think the company is riding on at this point).

On the actual FSD front, Musk was light on details on the call. He implied that the latest generation of FSD would have 10x more parameters. But he did not say when the latest generation of FSD would see more widescale adoption outside of cities like Austin Texas.

On the whole, I already touched on the tough delivery numbers for the EV giant. Automotive revenues were down 16% YoY and total revenues were down 12%. GAAP Gross margin declined from 18% last year to 17.2%.

Tesla Is Distracted

I strongly believe that Tesla is distracted and their key tech (FSD) is falling behind. I don’t think it’s a coincidence that these efforts coincide with declining delivery numbers.

I’m not the only one who has observed softer demand in the U.S. and Europe linked to backlash against Musk’s polarizing stances - and even veteran supporters are urging restraint.

It’s important to note that Musk himself, in my opinion, stretching himself too thin. Musk is now involved with SpaceX, Neuralink, The Boring Company, xAI, X (formerly Twitter) and Tesla. While he has been infamous for his ability to multitask, I think there are simply too many things Musk is focused on now, and I don’t think this is the best situation for Tesla. His attention is divided among so many different endeavors, and Tesla is only getting a small share of that.

Already, leaked recordings show that Tesla employee morale is falling. And this is before the tough period the company will need to go through in order to ramp up FSD.

FSD (full self-driving) remains in beta, and promised robotaxi launches slipped past 2024. Meanwhile, competitors like Google's (GOOG) Waymo are deploying real driverless taxis, and Musk’s controversies only invite regulatory scrutiny.

In fact, the taxi service that Tesla launched in San Francisco (where Waymo is operating autonomously) is ran with chauffeurs, not FSD. This is a huge sign their tech is not ready for primetime like I was lead to believe in earlier research.

Valuation

Seeking Alpha Quant gives Tesla an overall valuation grade of an F. While I was a strong buy before, I think this is reasonable now. When we look even closer at the valuation chart, we can see that Tesla has premium multiples on just about every key valuation metric at a time when revenue and EPS are sliding lower. At the time of this writing, Tesla shares are worth roughly $318. But when we look at the company’s forward Non-GAAP price-to-earnings ratio, we can see that Tesla’s forward P/E of 190.14 is ~972% higher than the sector median of 17.73.

On a similar note, Tesla has a forward price-to-sales ratio of 11.18, which is over 1,000% higher than the sector median of just 0.95. The company’s forward EV/EBITDA ratio of 74.68 is a stark 602% premium to the sector median, which is at just 10.63.

Interestingly, Tesla’s metrics exceed their own five-year averages by anywhere between 20-45%. To me, this indicates a re-inflated bubble from late 2023 and early 2024. In my opinion, any delivery or margin miss will trigger a sharp derating, given zero margin for error baked into the stock price.

If we compare this to General Motors (GM) (who I now this is executing well) the valuation differences are stark. GM trades at only a 5.77x forward P/E ratio despite stronger revenue growth over the trailing 12 months of 5.34% vs. Tesla's -2.73% YoY change in revenue.

Current prices are not reflecting this I think. I am a hold for now (so I do not know what the fair value is). I just think there is a big risk to the downside now.

Bull Thesis

To be clear, I am double downgrading shares of Tesla, there is a bull thesis that could be a diamond in the rough. Again, if Tesla can fix its FSD system, I do believe that there’s a lot of potential with Tesla’s self driving division. If Tesla can crack full self-driving at scale, robotaxi services and FSD licensing could become software-like revenue streams. FSD Beta v12’s pure vision model leverages about 50 billion miles of shadow-mode data versus Waymo’s ~70 million autonomous miles.

In fact, I think that Tesla has an AI and data edge. Tesla’s camera-only approach promises global scalability without expensive lidar or pre-mapping - validating its lofty valuation if robotaxis generate ~$30k gross profit per car annually. The question here is the “if” and I think this leads back to the company's distraction (and now falling employee morale).

Tesla’s competitor General Motors shuttered its autonomous driving division, Cruise, last year after the company failed to develop the autonomous driving tech it needed to scale an EV fleet.

Cruise’s co-founder, Kyle Vogt mentioned in a podcast recently that Tesla actually is doing the basics of their autonomous driving correctly. Tesla is focusing on a deep neural network.

But, as I am trying to show throughout this research, I think the company is distracted on what is a key nuanced R&D project. The company is distracted at one of the most pivotal moments in their history. Vogt notes that the current autonomous driving tech that Tesla has is not sustainable. This news is hot off the press (the podcast). It shows that as we see Tesla’s autonomous driving tech attempt to scale, they have serious drawbacks.

This is why diving into how their focus is distracted is so urgent. Tesla’s autonomous driving tech should be a “ChatGPT moment” for the company's trajectory. I don’t think they are set up that way right now.

Takeaway

I think it's clear as we look at the first half of 2025 in the rear view mirror (no punt intended), Tesla’s growth momentum has faltered and this downturn was more than just a blip. Margins are under pressure, and their focus remains frankly uncertain. However, the stock continues to trade at exceptional valuation premiums, which could be viewed as both a good and a bad thing.

In my opinion, investors should avoid adding new positions, and I believe that they should consider trimming gains. While Q2 earnings were relatively mute for the company’s share price, I’m worried now that this is a case of “waiting for the other show to drop.” Margins are tight and getting more compressed. Headwinds are not helping either.

With this, shares of Tesla are double downgraded from a strong buy to a hold.

data.title: Investors Tune Out The Political Noise As The Fed Holds
data.description:
Introduction

Yesterday, the Federal Reserve surprised nobody by maintaining the federal funds rate at 4-1/4 to 4-1/2 percent. The committee pointed out that while labor market conditions remained strong, inflation was continuing to be somewhat elevated. While the vote of 9-2 suggests some dissent on the committee, and investors will likely be bombarded with the political fallout from the decision, it is important to take stock in current economic data and the outlook for between now and the next Fed meeting.

The Calm of the Fed Fund Futures

Heading into the meeting, investors were not buying into the political rhetoric or the possibility of unexpected turnover at the Federal Reserve. The Fed fund futures market, which bets on the future of Fed funds rates, remained rather steady from the prior meeting on June 12 th into yesterday. Even looking out to the July 2026 futures, rates have stayed within a 40-basis point band since Liberation Day.

The markets are also aligned with the near-term outlook of the Federal Reserve Open Market Committee (FOMC), which published its expectations in June. The Fed futures curve closely matches the committee’s expectations through the end of the year, before projecting between one or two more cuts in 2026 that will carry into 2027.

Economic Growth Has Achieved Its Soft Landing

Just before the meeting yesterday, the BEA released the second quarter GDP figures, which showed that the economy exceeded expectations and grew by an annualized 3.0% in the second quarter after adjusting for inflation. The economy has growth figures of or higher than 3% in three of the last five quarters.

While the BEA takes quarterly data and annualizes it, I believe the best way to assess economic growth is to take year over year growth figures and calculate the changes. If we do that over the last two years, year over year growth comes in at 1.99% in each quarter, respectively. Essentially, the economy, after adjusting to inflation, is growing at the soft-landing target set by the Federal Reserve.

Inflation and Unemployment: The Key Indicators

Stable inflation with full employment remains the key objectives of the Federal Reserve. While the Fed continues to expect both metrics to go in the wrong direction later this year, economic data continues to defy the pessimism. Just this morning, the June PCE inflation data was released showing that year over year core inflation remained at 2.8%. The Phillips curve has been little changed compared to a year ago and inflation is equidistant between the Fed’s 2025 and 2026 targets while unemployment is 0.4% lower than expectations. Each of these characteristics points to the Fed continuing to remain neutral, but closer to cutting than what their public hesitation may suggest.

Looking Ahead to the Next Fed Meeting

The next Fed meeting will conclude on September 17th, and I don’t believe we will have much more clarity on trade than we do now. Tariffs are set to go into effect for the countries that do not cut deals with the US on August 1st. The data surrounding the revenue on those tariffs will come in days before the Fed meeting, but it will be hard to discern whether that represents a forward twelve-month trajectory or not.

What will be significant in the September meeting is the Fed’s newest economic projections. The Fed made it clear in its June meeting that it saw both inflation and unemployment rising between now and the end of the year. They based their estimates on advanced data that the committee had been reviewing. Those projections have not arrived yet, and if they don’t by September, it will be interesting to see if (or how) the committee changes its outlook.

Conclusion

Investors are largely staying out of the political drama between the Federal Reserve and Washington DC. Hopefully, they will continue to do so over the next several weeks as more drama unfolds between meetings. Even if there is a change at chairman, there are eight other members on the committee that will need swayed to changing monetary policy. Until the Fed sees a soft landing on pricing, they are not likely to move further on interest rates.

data.title: Revisiting JEPQ: A Measured Update And Alternatives
data.description:
I am here to revisit the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (NASDAQ:JEPQ) for a few reasons. After I wrote last, there were a few comments that I thought were worth discussing. I may have been a little harsh on the Sell rating and want to revisit that. For which I will explore all use cases where JEPQ may work. I also wanted to show explicitly why there are better alternatives, so a comparison with peers (within and outside the NDX core underlying) will be presented. I will also show how my core proposition of buy and generate income from NDX works.

Performance Update

Since I last wrote, this is how JEPQ has shaped up. At the time of writing my last review, NDX was already on the way up, although it wasn't clear how far the rebound would go. As it turned out, we have had a powerful rebound. And how has JEPQ done in this strong reversal? This is not JEPQ's territory, a bull phase. So expectedly it has underperformed QQQ (a proxy for NDX) - ~9.5% versus ~16% in the index it writes options on. The last time NDX was up ~16% in August to November 2024, JEPQ had not lagged by as much. Investors who banked on that behavior to be the norm, should have had a reality check in the most recent rally. However, this is still expected behavior for a option income strategy - capped upside and I don't hold that against JEPQ. It is the lack of commensurate drawdown mitigation (as discussed in my original thesis) that sets me against JEPQ.

The Ultimate Alternative

The ultimate alternative for income seekers, I had touched upon in my earlier thesis is investing in the NDX through an ETF like QQQ (or better still QQQM for tax efficiencies) and harvest similar income as JEPQ. Why? Because through a vehicle like JEPQ, you are riding similar drawdown risks (bettered by a margin) and ultimately relying on growth of NDX to fund your income. And over a long term, the upsides are usually greater than the corrections. JEPQ by design captures a lot of the drawdown and falls short on capturing upsides.

I understand that DIY implementation of JEPQ with options is an overhead many will not be willing to sign up for. And I don't recommend that either. I am recommending a simple Buy and Hold QQQM and withdrawing income when required - lower overhead. You need income you withdraw! That's the ask. In fact, the approach allows for adjusting income withdrawals as per need and retains a possibility of keeping more on the table for capital appreciation. And believe me, as I will show later, it is the capital appreciation from the growth of the underlying that actually funds your payouts in JEPQ, not much the option layer as is the intention.

So, to withdraw income from an investment in QQQM versus the payouts you receive from JEPQ, the only factor of note is tax treatment. For FY 2024, the annual report notes only about ~$61m as qualified dividends out of a total distribution of ~$809m. That is around ~7.5% of the payouts receiving preferential tax treatment. The rest is taxed at ordinary income rates. On the contrary, a withdrawal from a lumpsum investment in QQQ is taxed at ordinary rated for withdrawals made within the first year. Beyond which more tax friendly capital gains rates apply. There is more to the tax advantages of a DIY withdrawal. Control over cashflow is one - you withdraw more or less as per requirement and don't incur forced tax consequences of a payout from JEPQ. You can also harvest tax efficiencies, offsetting withdrawals in down markets against gains.

I have replicated a similar payout as JEPQ below, on a lumpsum investment in QQQ at the start of May 2022. For a similar $100 investment in each route and similar income generated (where DIY withdrawals from QQQ is more tax efficient), I end up with a remaining capital of $130 against JEPQ's ~$109. That is a very high difference over just 3 years. And I haven't even considered a close to ~0.5% dividend payout annually from QQQ.

For those you may think, the DIY QQQ route is more volatile and should show more drawdowns, below is the NAV of both approaches compared. Yes, DIY QQQ withdrawals should in theory show wider drawdowns, but that's very little differential in favor of JEPQ when compared to the upside gains seen in QQQ.

Also, to see illustrate how the situation would have looked if NDX had not rallied as it did in the period analyzed (and that biasing my conclusions), I checked the total return behavior between July 2024 to May 2025. A period where the NDX was net flat. You will notice from the table above that despite yielding the same income, the QQQ DIY approach led to a capital invested change from ~$122 to ~$123 in the period, while the capital changed from $107 to $105 for JEPQ.

Evaluating some Use Cases and Advantages

Total returns with no significantly worse volatility profile beats most use cases in my mind. And my detailed calculations above should support why JEPQ does have better alternatives from a risk adjusted total returns perspective. But I am willing to check whether there are other use cases where JEPQ fits in.

  • Income Generation - There are Better Alternatives. The ultimate alternative being DIY withdrawals from QQQ.
  • Management Hassles - Yes. JEPQ auto pays investors. I can concede ground here. Although I am not suggesting an option DIY approach as the alternative. I am just recommending simply withdraw same as JEPQ from QQQ (or as required). If JEPQ still feels less management overheads despite tax inefficiencies and a worse total return profile, go for it.
  • Better Drawdown Management - The expectation that JEPQ should work better in a sideways or correcting market is not a myth, but overrated. In my view the option layer does more harm than good for long term investors.

To illustrate the option layer's effectiveness, I have plotted total returns of just holding the underlying versus owning JEPQ in a period between December 2024 to May 2025, where NDX was net flat. Observe how JEPQ, despite its theoretical option layer advantage, actually underperforms the underlying index. In some other flat markets, it may show the benefits of an option layer, but surely in real market conditions, the options do not offer certain structural advantage to rely upon. However, it does offer almost "certain" disadvantages in capturing upsides.

Also the drawdown management is hugely better. At the trough of the tariff sell-off, as seen in the chart above, I see 3-4 percentage point benefits at best. Not enough to make the ride less volatile than holding QQQ. And certainly not enough when we see the opportunity loss on rebounds.

As a long term investor, I should not be planning for a sideways or correction markets throughout anyway. Only in a corner use case of tactically employing JEPQ expecting such a market, does JEPQ still merit a Buy. Even then I find it hard to support a "timing the market" use case, that too diverting the objective from income (what it is designed for) versus tactical gains.

However, given the use cases of JEPQ in down markets and the drags of the alternative's management overhead, I am willing to revisit my earlier harsh Sell rating. My stance remains the same and I have outlined my concerns with the option income strategy as a general concept here. But I have been reviewing many other income ETFs since my Sell rating, and have been evaluating them within the boundaries of its known limitations. I have rated some of them as Buy because of relative performance within the option selling income strategy umbrella. As we will see in the subsequent section, JEPQ does better than a other few option income alternatives, and its rating should be in comparison to those other similar alternatives. Hence a rerating to "Hold" to be consistent with my own assessments and not penalize JEPQ out of proportion. Consider the rerating as a normalization exercise in relative grading rather than a change of stance.

Other Alternatives

First a comparison with other NDX based option strategies. I like QQQI a Buy because of its drawdown management. On those grounds, JEPQ should also make a Buy. But JEPQ has been extremely sluggish in the past tariff sell-off rebound. Hence a Hold looks a better rating - looking from a relative lens with peers.

JEPQ does far better than QYLD in total return terms, including the drawdown management bit. QYLD gains a percentage point at time in choppy markets, but JEPQ is still the better income ETF to lean into.

JEPQ also turns out better compared to QYLG and QQQY. QQQY in fact suffers from a very high yield (mostly funded through a return of capital) that leaves not enough on the table for future growth. Overall, among the peer group, JEPQ wins most of the total return battles, holds on in terms of drawdown management. But its tax efficiencies are still poorer on average, within the pack. QQQI could be a better bet overall, including tax efficiencies.

Closing Thoughts

For income investors willing to explore outside the NDX and really agnostic about where the income is derived from, I have come across a few great alternatives that can be explored. LVHI is a great choice benefiting from international diversification and delivering lower dividends, but for really lower volatility. ADX balances income with growth really well. GPIX does the same strategy but places the option layer more dynamically and ends up doing quite well (albeit on a different index - the S&P 500). I would strongly recommend RDTE, a similar strategy but on a more conducive small cap space. QDVO is the closest to JEPQ in principle and gets many things right.

Closing on the thesis, I believe income focus has many better alternatives even within an option strategy framework. My best bet is not to go there at all though. However, I will rerate JEPQ to Hold as I feel I was unusually harsh on it and wanted to convey the message of how better alternatives exist. I think that is still true, but JEPQ is definitely not the worst ETF within its peer group (NDX based option writing ETFs and income ETFs overall). Some readers did point out that JEPQ does become the outperformer consistently in a lost decade like scenario. That could be true. But you enter an income plan on equities thinking probabilities and payoffs. The probability of the markets growing in the long term are definitely higher at any given point of time. And the pay-off in a JEPQ like instrument even in a lost decade is not significant beyond a few percentage point. Sometimes, even that is missing from it's performance.

data.title: STAG Industrial: Monthly Dividend Stock To Watch
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

STAG Industrial (NYSE:STAG) is a REIT focused on owning and operating industrial properties across the United States. The stock is down in the past year as a result of the environment, but their monthly dividend combined with a potential recovery in the environment could make this an attractive opportunity.

We see significant jumps in rent in recent years, with the Q2 YTD number standing at a 26.1% cash rent increase (1.6 million of the 4.2 million square feet of leases commencing in Q2 were new leases). This is not only STAG, but a relatively common trend in the industrial segment, especially thanks to the e-commerce growth and sustained demand following the pandemic. This is actually a very relevant point to remember, because it directly impacts their future rent, meaning higher revenue and funds from operations, which should translate into higher dividends.

As I’ll mention below, this “higher dividends” point is actually a problem with STAG, but I have to note that their growth in AFFO/EPS/etc. might now have been possible without their investments, which means less money for dividend increases. They’ve been investing significant amounts of money, with 576,764 square feet (for $61.7 million) acquired YTD, with a weighted average cash capitalization rate of 6.9%, while the number of shares outstanding has been relatively flat since 2022.

The company managed to develop thanks to strategic acquisitions in the past combined with the e-commerce boom caused by the pandemic, creating unprecedented demand for the type of industrial properties STAG owns, significantly boosting the activity and collection percentages. Also, they continued to develop on a per share basis even through the pandemic and high-rate environment, which is definitely impressive.

Financially, based on their latest report, we see a relatively common position for a REIT, with significant assets covering the total liabilities more than twice, but little-to-no current assets, as the company depends on the rent payments for interest/maturities. Again, this is common and is part of the business model of most REITs, with liquidity coming from rent, issuing stocks or debt or divestitures in some cases. We get $3.52 billion in equity on a $6.73 billion market cap, placing the PB at 1.91, but keep in mind that the balance sheet is backward-looking, and the real value of their properties in today’s market, when they are able to get significantly higher rent (see the double-digit increases mentioned earlier), means these assets could be worth more than what the company recorded them at back when they were acquired. If we look at it from an AFFO point of view, $3.52 billion is very little for $458.2 million generated last year, for example.

Still, there’s also the $396.8 million undrawn revolver balance if needed, as well as their committed private placement debt proceeds of $550 million (issuance announced back in April), bringing the total liquidity to an estimated $964 million. The debt maturities are also very well spread, with almost no debt left in 2025, $430 million in 2026 that will probably get refinanced and pushed into 2030+, and a weighted average interest of 4.13% on their debt, placing them in a good financial position.

Dividends & Buybacks

The dividend stands at ~4.15% today, which is among the company’s lowest in the past decade. This represents a ~60% payout ratio on their FFO, so there is potential to see them increase it if they want to.

However, that’s a big if. Now, I can understand limiting the dividend increases post-pandemic for some years, since the environment was very favorable for the industry overall and it’s better to be safe and capitalize on it than sorry, but the company was terrible at increasing its dividend even before that. 0.68% - 0.69% CAGR is far from beating inflation, and this yield is not really attractive in the current market compared to many other peers, as I’ll mention below. But again, they’re investing significantly in their growth and preparing for the future, so minimal dividend increases may be just a matter of time, until the management considers higher rates should suit them.

To be fair, as I’ll mention below, we are already in a time where the theoretically risk-free US treasuries pay better yields than many REITs, so there’s not that much of a reason for them to “fight” that to attract shareholders if they’re very long-term oriented. The fact that they also didn’t issue that many shares during this time also kind of reinforces this idea for me, since I believe they’re most likely expecting rate cuts to result in better share prices as well, leading to a switch from investing using cash to investing through issuing highly valued stock instead.

Risks & Opportunities

As a REIT - especially one in the industrial sector - probably their highest potential near-term catalyst will be potential rate cuts. Not only will it help the company directly by reducing the cost of debt (either if they refinance or directly lower their variable interest payments) - with interest payments currently at a record $120.3 million in the past 12 months - but it would also help their clients. This way, it will be easier for companies to invest, while rate cuts would also boost the economy, improve spending, and the overall economic activity.

This reduction in interest will also result in higher funds available, allowing STAG to increase their dividend, potentially (and hopefully) at better rates than before.

Not only that, but that should also be beneficial for dividend stocks in particular, since the market would be required to risk up if it wants good yields. We’ve had US treasuries (which are considered the risk-free assets) trading at ~4-5% yields for so long, which should theoretically have a negative effect on the demand for dividend stocks, as money would chase the better risk-adjusted yields. This is why most dividend stocks increased back when the Fed started cutting, and some are now reverting due to the tariffs causing fear and uncertainty around inflation and the future of rate cuts. I don’t believe we’ll get back to a zero-rate environment any time soon, but a reduction in rates is nonetheless beneficial.

In the near future, I believe we should see rate cuts come, even if Powell stops them entirely (recently announced another pause as the economy’s growth slowed down, with no hint of a September cut). That’s because his term ends in May 2026, and President Trump will nominate a new chair for the Fed during that time. This time, I believe we could see him choose a person that would likely cut rates regardless of the economy, especially since Trump has been very vocal about the Fed needing to cut rates lately, despite saying the cuts from a year ago were a bad idea.

Still, even without rate cuts, we can see significant improvements for STAG already, with the company recently increasing their Core FFO outlook for this year to $2.48 - $2.52, as leasing momentum accelerates and development initiatives advance, with the CEO noting that the first half’s results exceeded their initial business plan. It’s impressive that the company has addressed 90.8% of the expected leased square feet already this year, with cash leasing spreads expected to be in the 24.5% area. Plus, the developments are advancing, with a new 95-5 joint venture for a 500,000 square foot warehouse that should come in Q2 2026 with a targeted 7.1% cash yield, adding to their future FFO.

Peer Comparison

Looking at their peers, we see quite an interesting industry, with Prologis (PLD) being by far the leader, with a more than 15 times higher market cap than STAG and the rest, while there are many players similar or smaller than STAG, such as First Industrial Realty Trust (FR), Terreno Realty Corporation (TRNO), EastGroup Properties (EGP), Rexford Industrial Realty (REXR), Americold Realty Trust (COLD) and LXP Industrial Trust (LXP), focusing either on secondary markets or only a small number of cities/states.

Although there’s no news or clear plans about this, I believe it’s not impossible to see PLD continue to buy competitors (STAG included) as they did in recent years, since they need very significant investments to move the needle because they are a more than $100 billion market cap company. Still, they seem to be focused on their international expansion now, but I wouldn’t count out the possibility of seeing STAG enter a merger or acquisition in the future.

On the dividend side, we see STAG somewhere in the middle of the industry when it comes to the yield, being the best in terms of consecutive years of growth and payout ratio and the only one paying monthly dividends, but the worst in terms of actual dividend growth.

Valuation

For the valuation, I’ll adjust last year’s AFFO a similar 4.17% growth rate as the Core FFO (their midpoint expected level), leading to $477.3 million in 2025, growing this at a CAGR of 4% in the next 5 years followed by 3.25% in the following 5, and a terminal growth rate of 2.5%. Assuming a WACC of 8% (in line with market estimates) and considering $3.04 billion in net debt plus 186.62 million shares outstanding, the resulting model yields an enterprise value of ~$9.37 billion. After subtracting net debt, this leads to an equity value of ~$6.33 billion, or an intrinsic value per share of about $33.93.

This is almost exactly the current level, although the WACC will remain a key factor in this. As rates get cut, we could also see this go to levels such as 7% in the near term (I’m assuming rates will not go back to zero soon), which can lead to prices in the $45 range, similar to what they were at a few years ago.

Conclusions

Overall, I believe STAG is a Hold now, even if there is potential to see the stock go up once rates are cut - same as with any other dividend stock - but I think there are better dividend stocks out there. The company is healthy, developing in a key industry for the future, but the dividend yield is not that attractive for a REIT, and I don’t think growing it at a faster rate would change this. Still, I believe that once rate cuts come, there is potential to see them switch to a higher focus on funding their expansion through low-cost debt and issuing highly valued stock instead of cash, allowing them to hike the dividend at better rates than before.
